**#31746** 3:29 PM ✅ **Removed r1 corruption workaround from SNOBOL4 main procedure**

With the stack frame allocation bug fixed, the workaround preventing normal procedure returns is no longer needed. The SNOBOL4 main entry point previously included an inline assembly infinite loop to avoid returning through the corrupted r1 register, which would jump to PC=0 and re-enter the program. The workaround comment explicitly mentioned "some plsw runtime call (currently under investigation) corrupts r1"—the exact issue resolved by fixing large stack frame allocation. Now that procedures with frames exceeding 127 bytes correctly use sub sp,N instead of the wrapping add sp,-N instruction, the saved r1 return address remains intact. The main procedure can safely return through the standard epilogue, and the emulator properly detects program completion. This cleanup demonstrates confidence that the root cause is resolved and normal control flow is restored.

Read: ~354, Work: 🛠️ 1,959

**General**
| ID | Time | T | Title | Read | Work |
|----|------|---|-------|------|------|

**#31748** " 🔴 **Confirmed normal procedure returns work correctly after removing workaround**

Final verification confirms the stack frame fix completely resolves the r1 corruption issue. With the infinite loop workaround removed, the SNOBOL4 interpreter now relies solely on standard procedure epilogues for program termination. Both FizzBuzz and factorial tests execute successfully and report clean halts, proving that the main procedure's return address in r1 remains uncorrupted throughout execution. The emulator's "Halted: true" status indicates proper program completion rather than infinite looping or crashes. The binary size reduction (26512→26510 bytes) reflects removal of the assembly workaround instructions. This end-to-end testing demonstrates that procedures with large stack frames (204 bytes) now correctly preserve all saved registers including the critical r1 return address, enabling normal control flow and clean program termination across the entire SNOBOL4 implementation.

**#32168** 8:04 PM 🔵 **UART runtime stub architecture identified for RX implementation**

The session investigated how to implement UART receive functionality by examining the existing transmit-side implementation. The PLSW compiler (src/main.c) generates runtime stubs through C functions like emit_runtime_uart_putchar() that emit assembly code line-by-line. The existing _UART_PUTCHAR stub polls status register 0xFF0101 for bit 7 (TX busy) before writing to data register 0xFF0100. The requested _UART_GETCHAR needs to follow the same pattern but poll bit 0 (RX ready) instead. This investigation provides the template needed to add the receive-side counterpart, which will enable the downstream SNOBOL4 interpreter to read live keyboard input for interactive sessions.

**#32169** " 🟣 **UART_GETCHAR runtime stub implemented for receive functionality**

The PLSW compiler now generates a UART receive runtime stub to enable reading keyboard input from the COR24 emulator. The new emit_runtime_uart_getchar() function emits assembly code that implements _UART_GETCHAR, which polls the UART status register at 0xFF0101 for bit 0 (RX ready). When set, it reads the received byte from data register 0xFF0100 and returns it in r0. The implementation follows the same pattern as the existing _UART_PUTCHAR transmit stub, using standard prologue/epilogue with fp, r1, r2 save/restore. This completes the receive-side counterpart requested in GitHub issue #32, unblocking interactive input for the downstream SNOBOL4 interpreter that will use this to implement live READ operations via cor24-run --terminal mode.

The UART_GETCHAR runtime stub has been fully integrated into the PLSW compiler pipeline. The compile_program() function now emits the new receive stub alongside the existing transmit stubs when generating assembly output. The emission order places UART_GETCHAR between UART_PUTCHAR and UART_PUTS, maintaining consistency with the runtime architecture. The updated compiler built cleanly with the tc24r toolchain, confirming the implementation is syntactically correct. PLSW programs can now use UART_GETCHAR to read bytes from the COR24 UART, enabling the downstream SNOBOL4 interpreter to implement interactive input via the cor24-run --terminal mode as specified in the original issue.

content.text: Codegen already treats OTHERWISE as optional. Bug must be in the parser.

Found it. `NODE_NULL = -1` but `nd_alloc` initializes `nd_ival = 0`. When SELECT has no OTHERWISE, `nd_ival[n]` stays at 0 — which is a valid node index — so `cg_select` runs `cg_stmt(0)` on whatever happens to be node 0. Fix is in the parser.

content.content:
# PL/SW Compiler -- Build System
# Builds plsw compiler (C via tc24r) for COR24 emulator

tc24r_include := env("HOME") / "github/sw-vibe-coding/tc24r/include"
main_c := "src/main.c"
main_s := "build/plsw.s"

# Build the compiler
build:
    tc24r {{main_c}} -o {{main_s}} -I {{tc24r_include}} -I src

# Build and run interactively
run: build
    cor24-run --run {{main_s}} --terminal --echo --speed 0

# Build and run with UART input string (supports \n escapes)
run-input input: build
    cor24-run --run {{main_s}} --speed 0 -u "{{input}}"

# Build and run with cycle limit (for testing)
test: build
    cor24-run --run {{main_s}} --terminal --speed 0 -n 100000000

# Compile and run a .plsw program end-to-end
# Usage: just pipeline examples/hello.plsw
#        just pipeline examples/greet.msw examples/hello_macro.plsw
pipeline *args: build
    ./scripts/pipeline.sh {{args}}

# Compile a .plsw, save .s, and run with --dump for memory inspection
# Output: build/out.s (assembly) and build/run-dump.txt (memory dump)
pipeline-dump *args: build
    ./scripts/pipeline-dump.sh {{args}}

# Compile hello_macro example (macro demo)
hello-macro: build
    ./scripts/pipeline-dump.sh examples/greet.msw examples/hello_macro.plsw

# Compile chain example (control block demo with .msw includes)
chain: build

# PL/SW Compiler -- Build System
# Builds plsw compiler (C via tc24r) for COR24 emulator

tc24r_include := env("HOME") / "github/sw-vibe-coding/tc24r/include"
main_c := "src/main.c"
main_s := "build/plsw.s"

# Build the compiler
build:
    tc24r {{main_c}} -o {{main_s}} -I {{tc24r_include}} -I src

# Build and run interactively
run: build
    cor24-run --run {{main_s}} --terminal --echo --speed 0

# Build and run with UART input string (supports \n escapes)
run-input input: build
    cor24-run --run {{main_s}} --speed 0 -u "{{input}}"

# Build and run with cycle limit (for testing)
test: build
    cor24-run --run {{main_s}} --terminal --speed 0 -n 100000000

# Compile and run a .plsw program end-to-end
# Usage: just pipeline examples/hello.plsw
#        just pipeline examples/greet.msw examples/hello_macro.plsw
pipeline *args: build
    ./scripts/pipeline.sh {{args}}

# Compile a .plsw, save .s, and run with --dump for memory inspection
# Output: build/out.s (assembly) and build/run-dump.txt (memory dump)
pipeline-dump *args: build
    ./scripts/pipeline-dump.sh {{args}}

# Compile hello_macro example (macro demo)
hello-macro: build
    ./scripts/pipeline-dump.sh examples/greet.msw examples/hello_macro.plsw

# Compile chain example (control block demo with .msw includes)
chain: build
    ./scripts/pipeline-dump.sh include/cvt.msw include/ascb.msw include/asxb.msw include/tcb.msw examples/chain.plsw

# Clean build artifacts
clean:
    rm -f build/*.s build/*-combined.plsw build/*-dump.txt

content.text:
Fixed. Root cause: `nd_alloc` initializes `nd_ival = 0`, but `NODE_NULL = -1`. When SELECT parsed with no OTHERWISE, `nd_ival[n]` stayed at `0` — a valid node index — so `cg_select`'s check `nd_ival[node] != NODE_NULL` was true and it ran `cg_stmt(0)` on whatever happened to be node 0. That'd be the MAIN proc body in simple programs (hence sometimes "working") or arbitrary garbage elsewhere.

One-line fix at `src/parser.h:807` — set `nd_ival[n] = NODE_NULL` when allocating the SELECT node. Verified: SELECT without OTHERWISE now works for matching WHEN, falls through cleanly on no-match, and works inside non-MAIN procs. Existing `select_demo.plsw` (with OTHERWISE) still passes.

# /mw-cp — Mike Wright's Checkpoint Process

You are executing a disciplined checkpoint commit. Follow every phase in order.
Do NOT skip any phase, even if it seems clean. Report status after each phase.

---

## Phase 1 — Pre-Commit Checks

Run all of the following that apply to this project. Stop and report failures before proceeding.

1. **Format**: `cargo fmt --check` (or `rustfmt --check` on specific files)
2. **Lint**: `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`
3. **Build**: `cargo build` (debug is fine; surface any compile errors)
4. **Tests**: `cargo test` — all tests must pass
5. **Unused deps** (if `cargo-machete` present): `cargo machete`
6. **Project-local checks**: run `just check` or `just pre-commit` if a justfile exists

If any check fails, STOP. Report what failed and ask the user how to proceed.

---

## Phase 2 — Documentation Updates

Review recent changes and update the following as appropriate:

1. **CHANGELOG.md / CHANGES.md** — add an entry under `[Unreleased]` or today's date
2. **STATUS.md** — update current state, what changed, what's in progress
3. **LEARNINGS.md** — capture anything notable: decisions made, gotchas, tradeoffs
4. **Inline docs** — ensure any new public API items have doc comments (`///`)
5. **README.md** — update if any user-facing behavior, config, or setup changed

If none of these files exist, note that and skip gracefully.

---

## Phase 3 — Staged Diff Review

Run `git diff --staged` (or `git diff HEAD` if nothing staged yet).

Summarize what is changing:
- Files modified, added, deleted
- High-level functional description of the change

Then stage all changes: `git add -A`

---

## Phase 4 — Commit

Compose a detailed commit message following Conventional Commits style:
```
<type>(<scope>): <short imperative summary>

<body — explain WHAT changed and WHY, not HOW>
<mention any tradeoffs, alternatives considered, or follow-ups needed>

Refs: <issue/ticket numbers if applicable>
Co-authored-by: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
```

Types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `docs`, `test`, `chore`, `perf`, `build`

If  was provided, incorporate it as context for the message.

Execute: `git commit -m "<message>"`

---

## Phase 5 — Push

1. Check current branch: `git branch --show-current`
2. Check remote: `git remote -v`
3. Push: `git push origin <branch>`

If the push is rejected (non-fast-forward), STOP and report — do NOT force push without explicit user confirmation.

---

## Phase 6 — Checkpoint Summary

Report a brief summary:
- ✅ Checks passed
- 📝 Docs updated (list files)
- 🔖 Commit hash and message
- 🚀 Pushed to remote/branch

Flag any items that were skipped or need follow-up.
```

---
```

## Phase 4 — Commit

src/parser.h | 1 +
 1 file changed, 1 insertion(+)

src/parser.h | 1 +
 1 file changed, 1 insertion(+)

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
fix(plsw): make OTHERWISE optional in SELECT statements

The SELECT parser allocated a NODE_SELECT via nd_alloc() and relied on
nd_ival defaulting to NODE_NULL (-1) when OTHERWISE was absent. But
nd_alloc initializes nd_ival to 0, which is a valid node index. As a
result, cg_select's check `nd_ival[node] != NODE_NULL` was always true
for OTHERWISE-less SELECTs, and it ran cg_stmt(0) on whatever happened
to occupy node 0 -- typically the MAIN proc body, producing runaway
codegen in non-MAIN procs and stray output elsewhere.

Fix: explicitly set nd_ival[n] = NODE_NULL after allocating the SELECT
node so the "no OTHERWISE" case is detected correctly.

Verified: SELECT without OTHERWISE now works for matching WHEN, falls
through cleanly on no-match, and works inside non-MAIN procs. Existing
select_demo.plsw (with OTHERWISE) still passes.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

content.text: One open issue: #33 — compiler infinite loop on 96+ ELSE IF chains. Let me read the full details.

content.content:
title:	Compiler infinite loop / timeout on files with 96+ ELSE IF branches in a single chain
state:	OPEN
author:	softwarewrighter (Software Wrighter LLC)
labels:	bug
comments:	0
assignees:	
projects:	
milestone:	
number:	33
--
## Problem
The PL/SW compiler appears to enter an infinite loop (or at least exceed 2 billion COR24 instructions without producing output) when compiling a source file that contains approximately 96 or more `ELSE IF` branches in a single IF/ELSE IF chain.

## Reproduction
The file `sno_exec.plsw` in the `sw-cor24-snobol4` repo compiles successfully with 91 ELSE IF branches (the original) and 94 ELSE IF branches (after adding 3 more). However, when 5 additional ELSE IF branches were added (totaling ~96), the compiler runs to the 2B instruction limit without producing assembly output or any error message.

## Steps to reproduce
1. Take `src/sno_exec.plsw` from sw-cor24-snobol4 (commit c840e5d)
2. Add 5 new ELSE IF branches to the main `AM_EXEC` procedure's opcode dispatch chain (the `IF (OP = ...) THEN ... ELSE IF ...` block)
3. Compile with: `scripts/build.sh include/descr.msw include/heap.msw include/am.msw include/pat.msw include/snoglob.msw src/sno_exec.plsw`
4. The compiler produces no assembly output and runs until the instruction limit

## Expected behavior
The compiler should either:
- Successfully compile the file, or
- Produce a clear error message indicating the nesting/chain depth limit was exceeded

## Workaround
Keep the ELSE IF chain count under ~94 per procedure. Merged some conditions using OR to reduce the branch count.

## Impact
This limits the number of opcodes the SNOBOL4 AM executor can handle in a single dispatch loop. The current workaround (merging ELSE IF conditions) is adequate but fragile — adding more opcodes in the future may hit this limit again.

content.content:
#!/bin/bash
# pipeline.sh -- End-to-end PL/SW compilation and execution pipeline
#
# Usage: ./scripts/pipeline.sh [macro.msw ...] program.plsw
#
# When .msw files are provided, uses the FILE:/SOURCE: protocol so the
# compiler's %INCLUDE mechanism resolves them by name. Without .msw files,
# uses legacy mode (raw source + EOT).
#
# Requires: cor24-run, the compiler assembly at build/plsw.s

set -euo pipefail

COMPILER_ASM="${COMPILER_ASM:-build/plsw.s}"

if [ $# -lt 1 ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 [macro.msw ...] program.plsw" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Verify compiler exists
if [ ! -f "$COMPILER_ASM" ]; then
    echo "Error: compiler not built. Run 'just build' first." >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Separate .msw and .plsw files
MACROS=()
MAIN=""
for f in "$@"; do
    case "$f" in
        *.msw) MACROS+=("$f") ;;
        *.plsw) MAIN="$f" ;;
        *) echo "Error: unknown file type: $f" >&2; exit 1 ;;
    esac
done

if [ -z "$MAIN" ]; then
    echo "Error: no .plsw file specified" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Build UART input string with \n escapes for cor24-run -u
# Uses FILE:/SOURCE: protocol when .msw files present, legacy otherwise.
build_input() {
    printf 'c\\n'
    if [ ${#MACROS[@]} -gt 0 ]; then
        # FILE: protocol -- each .msw becomes a named include
        for m in "${MACROS[@]}"; do
            printf 'FILE:%s\\n' "$(basename "$m")"
            while IFS= read -r line; do
                printf '%s\\n' "$line"
            done < "$m"
            printf '\\x1E'  # record separator = end of file content
        done
        printf 'SOURCE:\\n'
    fi
    while IFS= read -r line; do
        printf '%s\\n' "$line"
    done < "$MAIN"
    printf '\\x04'
}

INPUT=$(build_input)

# Compile: run source through the PL/SW compiler on the emulator
COMPILER_OUT=$(cor24-run --run "$COMPILER_ASM" -u "$INPUT" -n 200000000 -t 120 --speed 0 2>&1)

# Extract the UART output block (multiline: from "UART output:" to "Executed")
UART_OUT=$(echo "$COMPILER_OUT" | sed -n '/^UART output:/,/^Executed /{/^Executed /d;p;}' | sed '1s/^UART output: //')

if echo "$UART_OUT" | grep -q "compilation failed\|COMPILE ERROR\|ERROR:"; then
    echo "Compilation failed:" >&2
    echo "$UART_OUT" | grep -E "ERROR:|failed" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Extract assembly between markers
START_MARKER="--- generated assembly ---"
END_MARKER="--- end assembly ---"
ASM=$(echo "$UART_OUT" | sed -n "/$START_MARKER/,/$END_MARKER/{/$START_MARKER/d;/$END_MARKER/d;p;}")

if [ -z "$ASM" ]; then
    echo "Error: no assembly output found in compiler output" >&2
    echo "Compiler output (last 20 lines):" >&2
    echo "$UART_OUT" | tail -20 >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Write assembly to temp file
TMPASM=$(mktemp /tmp/plsw-XXXXXX.s)
trap "rm -f $TMPASM" EXIT
echo "$ASM" > "$TMPASM"

ASM_LINES=$(echo "$ASM" | wc -l | tr -d ' ')
echo "=== Compiled $(basename "$MAIN") ($ASM_LINES lines of assembly) ===" >&2

# Show registered includes if any
echo "$UART_OUT" | grep "registered:" >&2 || true

# Run the generated assembly
echo "=== Running ===" >&2
RUN_OUT=$(cor24-run --run "$TMPASM" -n 10000000 -t 30 --speed 0 2>&1)

# Extract program output (multiline UART output block)
PROG_OUT=$(echo "$RUN_OUT" | sed -n '/^UART output:/,/^Executed /{/^Executed /d;p;}' | sed '1s/^UART output: //')

if [ -n "$PROG_OUT" ]; then
    echo "$PROG_OUT"
else
    echo "(no output)" >&2
fi

content.content:
author:	softwarewrighter
association:	member
edited:	false
status:	none
--
## Root cause analysis

Tracked down the issue by reading the PL/SW source. The bug is in the SELECT/WHEN parser at `src/parser.h` line 815:

```c
w = nd_alloc(NODE_DO_WHILE);  // WRONG node type!
nd_left[w] = wcond;
nd_right[w] = wbody;
nd_append(n, w);
```

Each WHEN clause is being allocated as `NODE_DO_WHILE` instead of a proper `NODE_WHEN` node type. When codegen later iterates the SELECT's children via `cg_stmt()`, it doesn't have a handler for these mis-typed nodes (the dispatch table at codegen.h line 1589 only handles NODE_ASSIGN/NODE_SELECT/NODE_RETURN/NODE_CALL/NODE_BLOCK/NODE_IF/NODE_DO_WHILE/NODE_DO_COUNT/NODE_DCL/NODE_ASM_BLOCK at the statement level).

When OTHERWISE is present, the codegen path changes and apparently routes through different code that masks the issue.

## Reproducer (current PL/SW HEAD: e7d4805)

This still fails on the latest compiler:

```
DCL X INT STATIC INIT(5);
MAIN: PROC;
    SELECT;
        WHEN (X = 3) CALL UART_PUTCHAR(65);
        WHEN (X = 5) CALL UART_PUTCHAR(66);
    END;
END;
```

Adding `OTHERWISE CALL UART_PUTCHAR(63);` before END makes it compile successfully.

The same failure occurs whether the WHEN body is a single statement (`WHEN (X = 3) CALL ...`) or a DO block (`WHEN (X = 3) DO; CALL ...; END;`). It's not body-related — it's the missing OTHERWISE.

## Suggested fix

Either:
1. Introduce a `NODE_WHEN` node kind and handle it in the SELECT codegen, or
2. Make the WHEN body handling consistent regardless of OTHERWISE presence (so the same code path handles both cases).
--

Tracked down the issue by reading the PL/SW source. The bug is in the SELECT/WHEN parser at `src/parser.h` line 815:

```c
w = nd_alloc(NODE_DO_WHILE);  // WRONG node type!
nd_left[w] = wcond;
nd_right[w] = wbody;
nd_append(n, w);
```

content.content:
435 docs/usage.md
End-to-end (per `docs/linker-design.md`):

1. Compile each `.plsw` to `.s` (libraries with `%DEFINE LIBRARY;`)
2. `meta-gen prep` -- rewrite external `la` refs to placeholders
3. Pass-1 `cor24-run --assemble` at base 0 -- get sizes and `.lst`
4. `meta-gen emit` -- produce `.meta` (EXPORT/FIXUP) from pass-1 `.lst`
5. Compute layout (entry module first)
6. Pass-2 `cor24-run --assemble --base-addr` -- internal refs at
   final addresses
7. `link24` -- combine binaries, patch FIXUPs
8. `cor24-run --load-binary` -- execute

Worked example: `components/linker/tests/demo-plsw-modular.sh`
exercises the full pipeline on a 2-module fixture under
`components/linker/tests/fixtures-plsw/` (`main.plsw` + `lib.plsw`
with `%DEFINE LIBRARY;`). The SNOBOL4 interpreter
(`sw-cor24-snobol4`) uses the same flow at scale via its
`scripts/build-modular.sh`.

## Error Messages

Errors include source line numbers:

```
SYNTAX ERROR line 3: expected expression
SYNTAX ERROR line 5: unexpected token
  expected ;, got END
CODEGEN ERROR: undefined variable for store
STORAGE ERROR: ...
```

## Error Messages

input.new_string:
## Error Messages

Errors include source line numbers:

```
SYNTAX ERROR line 3: expected expression
SYNTAX ERROR line 5: unexpected token
  expected ;, got END
CODEGEN ERROR: undefined variable for store
STORAGE ERROR: ...
```

## Known Limitations

### Long `IF / ELSE IF` chains — use `SELECT` instead

The parser and code generator handle `IF / ELSE IF` as right-nested
recursion: each `ELSE IF` becomes a nested `NODE_IF` inside the parent's
else branch. Because PL/SW itself runs on COR24 (8 KB EBR stack), a
single chain of roughly 90+ `ELSE IF` branches exhausts the host
compiler's call stack during parse or codegen. The symptom is that the
compiler produces no assembly output and either resets or runs to the
cycle limit -- no explicit error is reported. See issue #33.

**Workaround -- use `SELECT / WHEN` for dispatch-style code:**

```plsw
/* Instead of a long ELSE IF chain: */
SELECT;
    WHEN (OP = 1) CALL DO_ADD();
    WHEN (OP = 2) CALL DO_SUB();
    WHEN (OP = 3) CALL DO_MUL();
    /* ...hundreds more WHEN clauses are fine... */
    OTHERWISE CALL BAD_OPCODE();
END;
```

`SELECT` iterates its `WHEN` children in a loop inside `cg_select`, so
it does not recurse per branch and has no practical limit on the number
of `WHEN` clauses. `OTHERWISE` is optional -- omitting it means "no
action when no `WHEN` matches."

The SNOBOL4 interpreter (`sw-cor24-snobol4`) uses this pattern for its
AM opcode dispatch.

content.text:
## Summary

**Open issues:** just one (#33 — ELSE IF chain limit). No "next" open issue exists — #34 (SELECT/OTHERWISE) was already closed by the fix I committed earlier.

**Action on #33:** documented as a known limitation per your direction.
- Root cause: IF/ELSE-IF is right-nested recursion in both `parse_stmt` and `cg_if`; ~90+ branches exhaust the 8 KB EBR stack of the host compiler. Not an infinite loop — the compiler resets or runs to the cycle limit, matching the reporter's symptoms.
- Added a "Known Limitations" section to `docs/usage.md` with the `SELECT / WHEN` workaround (which iterates in a single frame and has no practical branch limit).
- Commented on #33 with root-cause analysis, workaround, and doc pointer. Left open as a tracking ticket in case we want to rewrite IF codegen iteratively later.

Changes are unstaged (`docs/usage.md`) — want me to `/mw-cp` it?

---PLAN---
# PL/SW Modular Compilation (GitHub issue #28)

Implement LIBRARY mode in the PL/SW compiler so large programs (e.g.
the SNOBOL4 interpreter) can be split across separately compiled
modules and linked with the existing FIXUP-based link24 toolchain in
components/linker/.

Reference: docs/linker-design.md (authoritative — supersedes the
literal text of issue #28, which predates the FIXUP design and still
mentions jump tables / EXTERNAL keyword / -m prefix flag; none of
those are needed in the current design).

## Approach

LIBRARY mode is activated by a `%DEFINE LIBRARY;` directive at the
top of a module's source. When set, the compiler suppresses:

1. Runtime preamble (_start, _UART_PUTCHAR, _UART_PUTS)
2. The MAIN wrapper procedure
3. ALL top-level DCL data emission (BASED records, globals, arrays)

External symbol references are NOT marked at compile time. The
existing meta-gen prep tool (components/linker/) scans the .s output,
detects unresolved `la rN,_SYMBOL` references, rewrites them to
`la rN,0` placeholders, and records FIXUPs in a .meta file. link24
patches the placeholders at link time.

## Existing state

- components/linker/{link24,meta-gen} already implemented (Rust)
- components/linker/tests/demo-fixup.sh exercises the linker on
  hand-written .s fixtures
- Uncommitted working-tree changes in src/codegen.h and src/main.c
  already implement LIBRARY-mode suppression — these need review,
  testing, and commit
- Issue #30 (DEF_MAX overflow) just fixed in commit 72a7111

## Steps

1. Review and commit the in-progress LIBRARY mode suppression in
   src/main.c and src/codegen.h
2. Add a PL/SW-driven end-to-end multi-module test (small fixture)
   exercising compile -> meta-gen prep -> assemble -> meta-gen emit
   -> layout -> reassemble -> link24 -> run on emulator
3. Build the SNOBOL4 interpreter (sw-cor24-snobol4) modularly using
   the new flow; verify the dating-app demo runs
4. Update docs/usage.md with a "Modular compilation" section pointing
   at docs/linker-design.md
5. Close GitHub issue #28

content.content:
commit 0c97e76e0309d8d6e26041bc07a03c6dfe5d36d8
Author: Mike Wright <mike@softwarewrighter.com>
Date:   Mon Mar 30 13:18:24 2026 -0700

    docs(plsw): add project planning, architecture, and agentrail saga
    
    Establish the PL/SW compiler project with complete planning documentation:
    - PRD defining a freestanding PL/I-inspired systems language for COR24
    - Architecture doc with 7-stage compilation pipeline
    - Design decisions covering syntax, types, macros, codegen, inline ASM
    - 12-phase implementation plan from setup through demos
    - Usage guide with language reference and code examples
    - CLAUDE.md with agentrail session protocol
    - 40-step agentrail saga covering full v1 implementation
    
    PL/SW targets the COR24 24-bit RISC ISA, written in C (via tc24r),
    with a macro preprocessor (.msw files) inspired by IBM PL/S-family
    systems languages. The compiler runs natively on COR24 and emits
    human-readable .s assembly.
    
    Co-authored-by: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

diff --git a/.agentrail/plan.md b/.agentrail/plan.md
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..ccf7986
--- /dev/null
+++ b/.agentrail/plan.md
@@ -0,0 +1,334 @@
+# PL/SW Compiler -- Implementation Plan
+
+## Phase Overview
+
+| Phase | Description                     | Deliverable                              |
+|-------|---------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
+| 0     | Project setup and toolchain     | Build system, hello-world on emulator    |
+| 1     | Lexer and token infrastructure  | Tokenize PL/SW source                   |
+| 2     | Parser and AST                  | Parse core language constructs           |
+| 3     | Symbol table and type system    | Scoped symbols, type checking            |
+| 4     | Code generation basics          | Emit .s for simple programs              |
+| 5     | Procedures and calling conv.    | PROC, CALL, RETURN with stack frames     |
+| 6     | Control flow                    | IF/THEN/ELSE, DO WHILE, DO count        |
+| 7     | Records and arrays              | Level-based structures, array access     |
+| 8     | Pointers and addresses          | PTR type, address-of, dereference        |
+| 9     | Inline assembly                 | ASM DO blocks, naked procedures          |
+| 10    | Macro preprocessor              | .msw parsing, ?MACRO expansion, GEN      |
+| 11    | Demo programs and testing       | UART hello, LED toggle, service wrappers |
+| 12    | Polish and documentation        | Listings, error messages, docs           |
+
+---
+
+## Phase 0: Project Setup and Toolchain Validation
+
+### Step 0.1: Repository structure
+- Create `src/` for compiler C source
+- Create `test/` for test PL/SW programs
+- Create `examples/` for demo programs
+- Create build script for tc24r + cor24-run pipeline
+- Verify tc24r compiles a trivial C program
+- Verify cor24-run assembles and executes it
+
+### Step 0.2: UART I/O bootstrap
+- Write uart_putchar, uart_getchar, uart_puts, uart_getline
+- Write print_int for decimal output
+- Test: compiler shell prints banner and reads input
+
+### Step 0.3: String and memory utilities
+- Write strcmp, strcpy, strlen, memset, memcpy
+- Write simple arena allocator for compiler tables
+- Test: basic string operations work
+
+---
+
+## Phase 1: Lexer and Token Infrastructure
+
+### Step 1.1: Token types and keyword table
+- Define token types: DCL, PROC, IF, THEN, ELSE, DO, WHILE, END,
+  CALL, RETURN, ASM, IDENT, NUM, operators, punctuation
+- Keyword lookup table (DCL, DECLARE, PROC, IF, THEN, ...)
+- Test: identify keywords vs identifiers
+

content.text:
Investigation complete. Here's what I found:

## Saga inventory

**Sagas that ever existed (filesystem-verifiable):**

| Saga | Created | Steps | State | Location |
|------|---------|-------|-------|----------|
| `plsw-compiler` (v1) | 2026-03-30 12:50 | 67 | completed, archived | `.agentrail-archive/plsw-compiler-20260407T095901/` ✓ intact |
| `plsw-modular` (v2) | 2026-04-07 09:59 | 5 | completed | working tree only — **never committed** |

**No third saga exists anywhere.** I checked: working tree, archive dir, `~/.local/share/agentrail`, `~/Library/...`, every `saga.toml` on disk. Only two saga.toml files exist; only one has ever been committed.

## v2 plsw-modular step → commit mapping (all accounted for)

| Step | Step mtime | Commit | Commit time |
|------|-----------|--------|-------------|
| 001-review-library-mode | Apr 7 10:05 | c5e7e5a (LIBRARY mode) | Apr 7 10:05 ✓ |
| 002-e2e-multimodule-test | Apr 7 14:34 | 964e42a (linker e2e test) | Apr 7 14:34 ✓ |
| 003-snobol4-modular-build | Apr 7 15:13 | (work in sw-cor24-snobol4 repo) | — |
| 004-document-and-close | Apr 7 15:23 | eb5fbcf (Modular Compilation docs) | Apr 7 15:22 ✓ |
| 005-005-fix-r1-corruption | Apr 7 15:29 | 0bf3ebe (sub sp,i24 / issue #31) | Apr 7 15:29 ✓ |

The single session file `5c5b1079...` covers all 5 steps, last write at 2026-04-07T22:29 UTC = Apr 7 15:29 PDT — exactly matches step 005's completion. The session only references `plsw-compiler` and `plsw-modular` — no third saga name.

## Commits after plsw-modular completed (no saga step exists)

| Commit | Time | Subject |
|--------|------|---------|
| 85a58f3 | Apr 7 17:25 | docs(readme): add Projects Using PL/SW section |
| e43f01a | Apr 8 20:05 | feat(runtime): add _UART_GETCHAR stub (issue #32) |
| e7d4805 | Apr 10 18:29 | feat(select): add SELECT/WHEN/OTHERWISE |
| b63ab15 | Apr 10 20:52 | fix(plsw): OTHERWISE optional (mine today) |

These four commits have **no corresponding step directory** in either the working tree or the archive, and no third saga.toml exists anywhere. Reflog is clean (no destructive ops, just commits and benign `reset HEAD` unstages).

## What the deletions in `git status` actually represent

The 67 deleted `D .agentrail/steps/...` and 35 deleted `D .agentrail/sessions/*.jsonl` lines in `git status` are just the **v1→v2 transition that was never committed**. When `agentrail archive` ran on Apr 7 09:59, it copied v1 to `.agentrail-archive/...` and cleared `.agentrail/` for the new saga. That cleanup is fully preserved in the archive — nothing was lost. It just looks alarming because the archive step never made it into a commit.

## Answer to your question

**No newer saga was deleted.** The deletions you're seeing are the v1→v2 archive transition (intact, just uncommitted).

The real anomaly is the opposite: the four commits between Apr 7 17:25 and Apr 10 18:29 (`85a58f3`, `e43f01a`, `e7d4805`) have no saga step backing them — either an agent skipped the saga protocol on those, or a v3 saga was deleted *without* archiving and left no filesystem trace at all. Given there's zero residual evidence (no archive dir, no orphan session file, no reflog anomaly, no third saga.toml ever in git history), I lean toward "agents worked outside the saga" rather than "v3 was deleted." But I can't prove it from filesystem evidence alone.

Want me to (a) commit the current `plsw-modular` archive transition so git stops showing 100+ phantom deletions, (b) start a fresh saga to track the post-modular feature work retroactively, or (c) something else?

Compare git history with saga history, report gaps

Walks first-parent git history on the current branch and matches commits to saga steps, either by the step's recorded commit hash (exact) or by timestamp window (heuristic fallback for legacy steps). Archived sagas' commits are treated as claimed and excluded from gap analysis.

Default output is a markdown report with four sections: matched, orphan commits (no matching step), orphan steps (no matching commit), and working-tree changes. With `--emit-commands`, prints a shell script of suggested `agentrail add` lines for orphan commits, pre-seeded from commit subjects. Review and edit the slugs and prompts before running the script.

Handles three cases: 1. Active saga with gaps: emits `add` lines for orphan commits. 2. No `.agentrail/` at all: emits `init --retroactive` followed by one `add` per historical commit, for bootstrapping an old repo. 3. Clean: reports no gaps.

Usage: agentrail audit [OPTIONS]

Options:
      --since <SINCE>
          Limit to commits after this git revision (e.g. "HEAD~50", "v1.0"). When omitted, scans all first-parent history on the current branch

      --emit-commands
          Emit a shell script of suggested `agentrail add` / `agentrail init` lines instead of a human-readable report. The script is a draft meant for human or agent review before execution — slugs and prompts are derived from commit subjects and usually need rewording

  -h, --help
          Print help (see a summary with '-h')
---
Save a point-in-time copy of .agentrail/ into the git object store

Belt-and-suspenders recovery aid. Creates a proper git commit under `refs/agentrail/snapshots/<timestamp>` containing a snapshot of `.agentrail/` (and `.agentrail-archive/` if present), then points a ref at it so the blobs are reachable and survive `git gc`. The user's real `.git/index` is never touched — snapshot operations run against a throwaway index via `GIT_INDEX_FILE`, so there are no staged-file side effects and no races with pre-commit hooks.

Intended use: run this manually before a risky agent operation, or after creating `.agentrail/` files you haven't yet committed, so that a stray `rm -rf` still has something to recover from. This is NOT a substitute for normal git tracking — it's a safety net for files that are not yet staged.

Restore from a snapshot with a normal git command: git restore --source=<ref> -- .agentrail .agentrail-archive

`agentrail snapshot --list` shows existing snapshot refs.

Usage: agentrail snapshot [OPTIONS]

Options:
      --list
          List existing snapshot refs instead of taking a new one

  -h, --help
          Print help (see a summary with '-h')
---
Write AGENTS.example.md — an agent instructions template for agentrail

Produces a self-contained Markdown file with the full session protocol, the rules for handling `.agentrail/`, and recovery guidance using `agentrail audit`. Drop it into any project that uses agentrail (rename to AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, .cursorrules, etc.) so that Claude Code, opencode, Cursor, and similar agents know how to use agentrail correctly.

By default writes `AGENTS.example.md` in the project directory; use `--output` to pick a different path. Refuses to overwrite an existing file unless `--force` is passed.

Usage: agentrail gen-agents-doc [OPTIONS]

Options:
      --output <OUTPUT>
          Path to write the template to (default: AGENTS.example.md in the project directory)

      --force
          Overwrite the target file if it already exists

  -h, --help
          Print help (see a summary with '-h')

1	# AGENTS.md
2	
3	Instructions for AI coding agents (Claude Code, opencode, Cursor, etc.)
4	working in this repository. This project uses **agentrail** to keep
5	multi-session agent work on track. Follow these rules exactly.
6	
7	Rename or copy this file to whatever your agent reads (`AGENTS.md`,
8	`CLAUDE.md`, `.cursorrules`, etc.) — the content is the same.
9	
10	---
11	
12	## The one-paragraph summary
13	
14	This project uses agentrail to record work as a sequence of **steps** in a
15	**saga**. Each session you run does exactly one step: read the step prompt
16	with `agentrail next`, start it with `agentrail begin`, do the work, commit
17	your changes with git, and close the step with `agentrail complete`. Then
18	stop — the next step is for the next session. The `.agentrail/` directory
19	is the durable record and must be tracked in git like source code.
20	
21	## The session protocol (follow exactly)
22	
23	### 1. START — read your instructions
24	
25	```bash
26	agentrail next
27	```
28	
29	This prints the current step's prompt, relevant context files, skill
30	documents, and past successful trajectories for this task type. **Read
31	the entire output carefully.** It is the instruction for this session.
32	
33	If `agentrail next` exits with no current step, the saga is paused or
34	complete — stop and ask the user what to do. Do not invent work.
35	
36	### 2. BEGIN — transition the step
37	
38	```bash
39	agentrail begin
40	```
41	
42	This marks the step as `in-progress`. Required before doing work.
43	
44	### 3. WORK — do exactly what the step prompt says
45	
46	- Do not ask the user "want me to proceed?" or "shall I start?". The
47	  step prompt **is** your instruction. Execute it.
48	- Do not expand scope. If you notice other problems, note them for a
49	  future step — do not silently fix them in this one.
50	- Stay within the files the step prompt references. If you need to touch
51	  something outside that scope, pause and ask.
52	
53	### 4. COMMIT — commit your work with git
54	
55	```bash
56	git add <files>
57	git commit -m "<clear message>"
58	```
59	
60	**This must happen before `agentrail complete`.** `agentrail complete`
61	captures the current `HEAD` commit hash into the step's `commits` field,
62	which is how future `agentrail audit` runs link the step back to its
63	commit. If you complete before committing, the linkage is wrong.
64	
65	Include `.agentrail/` files you touched in the commit — they are part of
66	the record.
67	
68	### 5. COMPLETE — close the step
69	
70	```bash
71	agentrail complete \
72	  --summary "what you accomplished in one or two sentences" \
73	  --reward 1 \
74	  --actions "tools and approach used"
75	```
76	
77	Flags:
78	- `--reward 1` on success, `--reward -1 --failure-mode "<what-went-wrong>"`
79	  on failure. Reward is used for trajectory recording so future sessions
80	  can learn from what worked.
81	- Add `--done` if this was the last step of the saga.
82	- Use `--next-slug` and `--next-prompt` to define the next step if you
83	  know what it should be; otherwise the human will plan it.
84	
85	### 6. STOP — do not continue
86	
87	**Do not make any further changes after `agentrail complete`.** Any
88	changes after complete are untracked by the saga and invisible to the
89	next session. If you see more work to do, it belongs in the next step,
90	not this one.
91	
92	---
93	
94	## Rules for `.agentrail/` (CRITICAL — do not violate)
95	
96	The `.agentrail/` directory is the durable record of saga/step history.
97	Treat it like source code.
98	
99	### Always track it in git
100	
101	- `.agentrail/` **must** be tracked in git. Never add it to `.gitignore`.
102	  If you inherit a repo that has `.agentrail/` ignored, that is a bug —
103	  unignore it and commit the existing contents first.
104	- Commit step artifacts as each step completes, in the same commit as
105	  your code changes.
106	
107	### Never edit or delete files under `.agentrail/` by hand
108	
109	- **Do not** `rm`, `rm -rf`, `mv`, or use `Write`/`Edit` on any file
110	  under `.agentrail/` or `.agentrail-archive/`.
111	- Always go through agentrail subcommands: `init`, `add`, `begin`,
112	  `complete`, `abort`, `archive`, `plan`, `audit`.
113	- Direct deletion of untracked step files is **unrecoverable** — git
114	  reflog cannot restore blobs that were never staged. This has happened
115	  before and lost saga history.
116	
117	### Commit order matters
118	
119	Work → `git add` → `git commit` → `agentrail complete`. In that order.
120	Completing before committing means `commits` is empty and the audit
121	command can't link step to commit.
122	
123	---
124	
125	## Recovering from gaps
126	
127	If history gets out of sync — for example, an agent made commits without
128	running `agentrail complete`, or steps were added without matching
129	commits — use the audit command.
130	
131	```bash
132	agentrail audit                    # human-readable markdown report
133	agentrail audit --emit-commands    # shell script of suggested add lines
134	agentrail audit --since v1.0       # only look at commits after v1.0
135	```
136	
137	The report has four sections:
138	
139	1. **Matched** — commits that line up with a saga step (by recorded hash
140	   or by timestamp window for legacy steps).
141	2. **Orphan commits** — commits with no matching step. These are the
142	   gaps.
143	3. **Orphan steps** — steps whose recorded commit isn't in the current
144	   history (rebased away, squashed, never made).
145	4. **Working tree** — uncommitted changes. Reported for awareness, not
146	   turned into commands.
147	
148	With `--emit-commands`, the tool prints a shell script with one
149	`agentrail add --commit <hash> --slug ... --prompt ...` line per orphan
150	commit. **Review and edit the slugs and prompts before running** — the
151	defaults are seeded from commit subjects and need human judgment.
152	
153	## Retroactive history for old projects
154	
155	If the project predates agentrail and you want to add a saga on top of
156	existing history:
157	
158	```bash
159	agentrail audit --emit-commands > rebuild.sh
160	# Edit rebuild.sh: reword slugs and prompts as coherent step descriptions
161	sh rebuild.sh
162	```
163	
164	The script begins with `agentrail init --retroactive --name development`
165	(when no saga exists) and then adds one step per historical commit.
166	Retroactive sagas are marked in `saga.toml` so future audits know those
167	commits are claimed.
168	
169	Going forward from there, run new sagas normally.
170	
171	## Safety net: `agentrail snapshot`
172	
173	If you have files under `.agentrail/` that are not yet committed and
174	you're about to do something risky (a big agent run, a rebase, cleaning
175	up untracked files), run:
176	
177	```bash
178	agentrail snapshot
179	```
180	
181	This creates a git commit under `refs/agentrail/snapshots/<timestamp>`
182	containing a copy of `.agentrail/` and `.agentrail-archive/`. The user's
183	real git index is not touched — it uses a throwaway temp index under the
184	hood. The snapshot survives `git gc` because a named ref holds it.
185	
186	Restore from a snapshot with a normal git command:
187	
188	```bash
189	git restore --source=refs/agentrail/snapshots/<timestamp> \
190	    -- .agentrail .agentrail-archive
191	```
192	
193	List existing snapshots with `agentrail snapshot --list`.
194	
195	This is a safety net, not a replacement for committing. Commit your
196	work normally — use snapshot only as belt-and-suspenders insurance.
197	
198	---
199	
200	## Quick reference
201	
202	| Command | When to use |
203	|---|---|
204	| `agentrail next` | Start of every session |
205	| `agentrail begin` | After reading `next`, before working |
206	| `agentrail complete --summary "..." --reward 1` | After committing |
207	| `agentrail status` | Inspect current saga state (read-only) |
208	| `agentrail history` | Show all step summaries (read-only) |
209	| `agentrail plan --update ...` | Revise the saga plan |
210	| `agentrail add --slug ... --prompt ...` | Add a step without completing current one (maintenance mode) |
211	| `agentrail abort --reason "..."` | Mark current step as blocked |
212	| `agentrail archive --reason "..."` | Close out a saga and start fresh |
213	| `agentrail audit` | Diagnose saga-vs-git gaps |
214	| `agentrail snapshot` | Save a safety-net copy of `.agentrail/` into the git object store (opt-in) |
215	| `agentrail snapshot --list` | List existing snapshot refs |
216	
217	## What not to do
218	
219	- Do not run `agentrail complete` before committing.
220	- Do not touch files under `.agentrail/` with anything other than
221	  `agentrail` subcommands.
222	- Do not keep working after `agentrail complete`. Stop and let the next
223	  session pick up.
224	- Do not add `.agentrail/` to `.gitignore`.
225	- Do not skip `agentrail next` "because you remember what the step was"
226	  — the next output includes trajectories and skill docs that change as
227	  the system learns.
228

# AGENTS.md

Instructions for AI coding agents (Claude Code, opencode, Cursor, etc.)
working in this repository. This project uses **agentrail** to keep
multi-session agent work on track. Follow these rules exactly.

Rename or copy this file to whatever your agent reads (`AGENTS.md`,
`CLAUDE.md`, `.cursorrules`, etc.) — the content is the same.

---

## The one-paragraph summary

This project uses agentrail to record work as a sequence of **steps** in a
**saga**. Each session you run does exactly one step: read the step prompt
with `agentrail next`, start it with `agentrail begin`, do the work, commit
your changes with git, and close the step with `agentrail complete`. Then
stop — the next step is for the next session. The `.agentrail/` directory
is the durable record and must be tracked in git like source code.

## The session protocol (follow exactly)

### 1. START — read your instructions

```bash
agentrail next
```

This prints the current step's prompt, relevant context files, skill
documents, and past successful trajectories for this task type. **Read
the entire output carefully.** It is the instruction for this session.

If `agentrail next` exits with no current step, the saga is paused or
complete — stop and ask the user what to do. Do not invent work.

### 2. BEGIN — transition the step

```bash
agentrail begin
```

This marks the step as `in-progress`. Required before doing work.

### 3. WORK — do exactly what the step prompt says

- Do not ask the user "want me to proceed?" or "shall I start?". The
  step prompt **is** your instruction. Execute it.
- Do not expand scope. If you notice other problems, note them for a
  future step — do not silently fix them in this one.
- Stay within the files the step prompt references. If you need to touch
  something outside that scope, pause and ask.

### 4. COMMIT — commit your work with git

```bash
git add <files>
git commit -m "<clear message>"
```

**This must happen before `agentrail complete`.** `agentrail complete`
captures the current `HEAD` commit hash into the step's `commits` field,
which is how future `agentrail audit` runs link the step back to its
commit. If you complete before committing, the linkage is wrong.

Include `.agentrail/` files you touched in the commit — they are part of
the record.

### 5. COMPLETE — close the step

```bash
agentrail complete \
  --summary "what you accomplished in one or two sentences" \
  --reward 1 \
  --actions "tools and approach used"
```

Flags:
- `--reward 1` on success, `--reward -1 --failure-mode "<what-went-wrong>"`
  on failure. Reward is used for trajectory recording so future sessions
  can learn from what worked.
- Add `--done` if this was the last step of the saga.
- Use `--next-slug` and `--next-prompt` to define the next step if you
  know what it should be; otherwise the human will plan it.

### 6. STOP — do not continue

**Do not make any further changes after `agentrail complete`.** Any
changes after complete are untracked by the saga and invisible to the
next session. If you see more work to do, it belongs in the next step,
not this one.

---

## Rules for `.agentrail/` (CRITICAL — do not violate)

The `.agentrail/` directory is the durable record of saga/step history.
Treat it like source code.

### Always track it in git

- `.agentrail/` **must** be tracked in git. Never add it to `.gitignore`.
  If you inherit a repo that has `.agentrail/` ignored, that is a bug —
  unignore it and commit the existing contents first.
- Commit step artifacts as each step completes, in the same commit as
  your code changes.

### Never edit or delete files under `.agentrail/` by hand

- **Do not** `rm`, `rm -rf`, `mv`, or use `Write`/`Edit` on any file
  under `.agentrail/` or `.agentrail-archive/`.
- Always go through agentrail subcommands: `init`, `add`, `begin`,
  `complete`, `abort`, `archive`, `plan`, `audit`.
- Direct deletion of untracked step files is **unrecoverable** — git
  reflog cannot restore blobs that were never staged. This has happened
  before and lost saga history.

### Commit order matters

Work → `git add` → `git commit` → `agentrail complete`. In that order.
Completing before committing means `commits` is empty and the audit
command can't link step to commit.

---

## Recovering from gaps

If history gets out of sync — for example, an agent made commits without
running `agentrail complete`, or steps were added without matching
commits — use the audit command.

```bash
agentrail audit                    # human-readable markdown report
agentrail audit --emit-commands    # shell script of suggested add lines
agentrail audit --since v1.0       # only look at commits after v1.0
```

The report has four sections:

1. **Matched** — commits that line up with a saga step (by recorded hash
   or by timestamp window for legacy steps).
2. **Orphan commits** — commits with no matching step. These are the
   gaps.
3. **Orphan steps** — steps whose recorded commit isn't in the current
   history (rebased away, squashed, never made).
4. **Working tree** — uncommitted changes. Reported for awareness, not
   turned into commands.

With `--emit-commands`, the tool prints a shell script with one
`agentrail add --commit <hash> --slug ... --prompt ...` line per orphan
commit. **Review and edit the slugs and prompts before running** — the
defaults are seeded from commit subjects and need human judgment.

## Retroactive history for old projects

If the project predates agentrail and you want to add a saga on top of
existing history:

```bash
agentrail audit --emit-commands > rebuild.sh
# Edit rebuild.sh: reword slugs and prompts as coherent step descriptions
sh rebuild.sh
```

The script begins with `agentrail init --retroactive --name development`
(when no saga exists) and then adds one step per historical commit.
Retroactive sagas are marked in `saga.toml` so future audits know those
commits are claimed.

Going forward from there, run new sagas normally.

## Safety net: `agentrail snapshot`

If you have files under `.agentrail/` that are not yet committed and
you're about to do something risky (a big agent run, a rebase, cleaning
up untracked files), run:

```bash
agentrail snapshot
```

This creates a git commit under `refs/agentrail/snapshots/<timestamp>`
containing a copy of `.agentrail/` and `.agentrail-archive/`. The user's
real git index is not touched — it uses a throwaway temp index under the
hood. The snapshot survives `git gc` because a named ref holds it.

Restore from a snapshot with a normal git command:

```bash
git restore --source=refs/agentrail/snapshots/<timestamp> \
    -- .agentrail .agentrail-archive
```

List existing snapshots with `agentrail snapshot --list`.

This is a safety net, not a replacement for committing. Commit your
work normally — use snapshot only as belt-and-suspenders insurance.

---

## Quick reference

| Command | When to use |
|---|---|
| `agentrail next` | Start of every session |
| `agentrail begin` | After reading `next`, before working |
| `agentrail complete --summary "..." --reward 1` | After committing |
| `agentrail status` | Inspect current saga state (read-only) |
| `agentrail history` | Show all step summaries (read-only) |
| `agentrail plan --update ...` | Revise the saga plan |
| `agentrail add --slug ... --prompt ...` | Add a step without completing current one (maintenance mode) |
| `agentrail abort --reason "..."` | Mark current step as blocked |
| `agentrail archive --reason "..."` | Close out a saga and start fresh |
| `agentrail audit` | Diagnose saga-vs-git gaps |
| `agentrail snapshot` | Save a safety-net copy of `.agentrail/` into the git object store (opt-in) |
| `agentrail snapshot --list` | List existing snapshot refs |

## What not to do

- Do not run `agentrail complete` before committing.
- Do not touch files under `.agentrail/` with anything other than
  `agentrail` subcommands.
- Do not keep working after `agentrail complete`. Stop and let the next
  session pick up.
- Do not add `.agentrail/` to `.gitignore`.
- Do not skip `agentrail next` "because you remember what the step was"
  — the next output includes trajectories and skill docs that change as
  the system learns.

Safety net in place. Now the audit + comparison + recommendations.

## Diagnosis from `agentrail audit`

| Section | Count | Detail |
|---------|-------|--------|
| Matched (commit ↔ step) | 4 | All plsw-modular steps that committed in *this* repo |
| Orphan commits | 5 | `72a7111`, `85a58f3`, `e43f01a`, `e7d4805`, `b63ab15` |
| Orphan steps | 1 | `003-snobol4-modular-build` (work was in sibling repo, no plsw commit — expected) |
| Uncommitted working-tree entries | 254 | The v1→v2 archive transition that was never committed |

**This confirms my prior finding:** there is no surviving evidence of a deleted v3 saga. The 5 orphan commits are real ad-hoc work that was never wrapped in a saga step. The 254 working-tree deletions are the v1 cleanup from `agentrail archive` that nobody ever committed.

## Gaps in your CLAUDE.md vs `AGENTS.example.md`

The most important rules **missing from CLAUDE.md** that would have prevented this situation:

| # | Missing rule | Why it matters here |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Never edit/delete files under `.agentrail/` by hand** (no `rm`, `mv`, `Write`, `Edit`) — always go through subcommands. The template explicitly says: "Direct deletion of untracked step files is **unrecoverable** — git reflog cannot restore blobs that were never staged. This has happened before and lost saga history." | This is exactly the situation you were worried about. CLAUDE.md says nothing about it. |
| 2 | **Commit `.agentrail/` files in the same commit as your code changes.** | The plsw-modular steps and the v1 archive transition were never committed → 254-line working tree drift, broken `agentrail audit` linkage. |
| 3 | **`.agentrail/` MUST be tracked in git, never `.gitignore`'d.** | Defensive — your repo is fine here, but the rule is missing. |
| 4 | **What to do when `agentrail next` reports no current step**: stop and ask the user. Do not invent work. | This is *exactly* what went wrong on Apr 7-10: after plsw-modular completed at step 5, three more commits happened (`85a58f3`, `e43f01a`, `e7d4805`) with no saga, because CLAUDE.md doesn't tell agents what to do when the saga is complete. |
| 5 | **Recovery via `agentrail audit` / `audit --emit-commands`** for diagnosing gaps. | Net-new feature; CLAUDE.md predates it. |
| 6 | **`agentrail snapshot`** as a safety net for uncommitted `.agentrail/` state. | Net-new feature; would have protected the modular saga state. |
| 7 | **`agentrail add` for ad-hoc / maintenance steps**, and `complete --next-slug --next-prompt` for chaining. | CLAUDE.md only documents the basic 6-step protocol. |
| 8 | **Stay within step scope** — don't expand. | CLAUDE.md says "don't ask permission" but doesn't say "don't expand scope". |

## Updated recommendations for next steps

I'd do these in order, with your confirmation at the marked points:

1. **Update CLAUDE.md** to import the missing rules above (especially #1, #2, #4). I can edit the file in place — preserves your existing structure, adds the missing sections. *(safe, local)*

2. **Commit the archive transition** so `git status` stops showing 254 phantom deletions. The plsw-modular saga state (steps + saga.toml + plan.md) and the .agentrail-archive/plsw-compiler-... directory all get added in one commit. The session file inside `.agentrail/sessions/` is from the archived saga's last session — it was already in HEAD before, will get re-added. *(local, reversible)*
   - **Confirm:** is `5c5b1079-...jsonl` (the one current session file) actually a session you want kept, or should it move into the v2 saga's sessions/ since it covers the modular work?

3. **Decide what to do about the 5 orphan commits.** Three options, ask which:
   - **(a) Leave them as orphans** — accept they were pre-saga / between-saga ad-hoc work, document this in CLAUDE.md going forward.
   - **(b) Retroactively add them to a new saga** like `plsw-maintenance` via `agentrail audit --emit-commands > rebuild.sh`, edit slugs/prompts, then run. This gives clean audit linkage going forward but requires reviewing/editing the generated commands.
   - **(c) Bind them to plsw-modular** by reopening the saga and adding steps 6-10 retroactively. Cleanest narrative ("modular work plus follow-on fixes") but the saga is already marked completed.

4. **Going forward:** start a new saga (`plsw-maintenance` or per-feature) before the next code work, so future commits map to steps automatically.

The snapshot at `refs/agentrail/snapshots/20260411T111735` covers everything currently in `.agentrail/` and `.agentrail-archive/`, so step 2 above is fully reversible. Tell me which of (a)/(b)/(c) you want for the orphan commits, and whether to start with the CLAUDE.md update.

message.content: proceed with 1 and 2, 3(b or c, your preference), 4 new saga maintenance

# sw-cor24-plsw -- Claude Instructions

## Project Overview

PL/SW (Programming Language for Software Wrighter) compiler for the
COR24 24-bit RISC ISA. A freestanding, PL/I-inspired systems language
with AST-aware compile-time macros, transpiling to COR24 assembler
source (.s). Written in C (via tc24r cross-compiler), targeting the
COR24 emulator.

See `docs/` for detailed documentation:
- `docs/prd.md` -- requirements
- `docs/architecture.md` -- system architecture
- `docs/design.md` -- design decisions
- `docs/plan.md` -- phased implementation plan
- `docs/usage.md` -- language reference and usage guide
- `docs/research.txt` -- PL/SW design research and rationale

## CRITICAL: AgentRail Session Protocol (MUST follow exactly)

### 1. START (do this FIRST, before anything else)
```bash
agentrail next
```
Read the output carefully. It contains your current step, prompt,
plan context, and any relevant skills/trajectories.

### 2. BEGIN (immediately after reading the next output)
```bash
agentrail begin
```

### 3. WORK (do what the step prompt says)
Do NOT ask "want me to proceed?". The step prompt IS your instruction.
Execute it directly.

### 4. COMMIT (after the work is done)
Commit your code changes with git. Use `/mw-cp` for the checkpoint
process (pre-commit checks, docs, detailed commit, push).

### 5. COMPLETE (LAST thing, after committing)
```bash
agentrail complete --summary "what you accomplished" \
  --reward 1 \
  --actions "tools and approach used"
```
- If the step failed: `--reward -1 --failure-mode "what went wrong"`
- If the saga is finished: add `--done`

### 6. STOP (after complete, DO NOT continue working)
Do NOT make further code changes after running `agentrail complete`.
Any changes after complete are untracked and invisible to the next
session. Future work belongs in the NEXT step, not this one.

## Key Rules

- **Do NOT skip steps** -- the next session depends on accurate tracking
- **Do NOT ask for permission** -- the step prompt is the instruction
- **Do NOT continue working** after `agentrail complete`
- **Commit before complete** -- always commit first, then record completion

## Useful Commands

```bash
agentrail status          # Current saga state
agentrail history         # All completed steps
agentrail plan            # View the plan
agentrail next            # Current step + context
```

## Build / Test

This project uses tc24r (C compiler) and cor24-run (emulator):

```bash
# Compile C to COR24 assembly
tc24r src/plsw.c -o build/plsw.s

# Assemble and run on emulator
cor24-run --run build/plsw.s
```

Stable toolchain references:
- `~/github/sw-vibe-coding/tc24r` -- C compiler source
- `~/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-emulator` -- emulator + assembler
- `~/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-tinyc` -- tc24r compiler (Rust)

Sibling language projects for reference:
- `~/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-apl` -- APL interpreter (C via tc24r)
- `~/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-pascal` -- Pascal compiler (C via tc24r)
- `~/github/sw-vibe-coding/tml24c` -- Macro Lisp compiler (C via tc24r)

## COR24 Quick Reference

- 24-bit RISC, 8 registers (r0-r2 GP, fp, sp, z, iv, ir)
- 1 MB SRAM + 8 KB EBR stack
- UART at 0xFF0100 (data), 0xFF0101 (status)
- LED at 0xFF0000
- No hardware divide -- use software division
- Variable-length instructions (1, 2, or 4 bytes)
- Calling convention: args on stack (R-to-L), return in r0, link via stack
- Prologue: push fp, push r2, push r1, mov fp,sp
- First arg at fp+9 (3 saved regs x 3 bytes)

## PL/SW Language Summary

- Source files: `.plsw`, macro files: `.msw`
- PL/I-like syntax: DCL, PROC, DO/END, IF/THEN/ELSE
- Integer-only types: BIT, BYTE, WORD, INT(n), CHAR, PTR
- Level-based structure declarations (1, 3, 5, ...)
- Macro invocation: `?MACRO(KEYWORD(value), ...)`
- Inline assembly: `ASM DO; "instr"; END;`
- No floats, no runtime, freestanding

input.new_string:
## CRITICAL: AgentRail Session Protocol (MUST follow exactly)

### 1. START (do this FIRST, before anything else)
```bash
agentrail next
```
Read the output carefully. It contains your current step, prompt,
plan context, and any relevant skills/trajectories.

**If `agentrail next` reports no current step (saga complete or
paused): STOP and ask the user what to do. Do NOT invent work.**
Don't go fix bugs, don't refactor, don't "just clean up" -- the
absence of a step is a signal that the next saga has not been
planned yet, and ad-hoc work between sagas creates orphan commits
that `agentrail audit` cannot reconcile.

### 2. BEGIN (immediately after reading the next output)
```bash
agentrail begin
```

### 3. WORK (do what the step prompt says)
Do NOT ask "want me to proceed?". The step prompt IS your instruction.
Execute it directly. Do NOT expand scope -- if you notice unrelated
problems, note them for a future step rather than silently fixing
them in this one.

### 4. COMMIT (after the work is done)
Commit your code changes with git. Use `/mw-cp` for the checkpoint
process (pre-commit checks, docs, detailed commit, push).

**Include any `.agentrail/` files you touched in the same commit
as your code changes** -- step.toml, summary.md, saga.toml, plan.md,
new step directories. They are part of the durable record and
`agentrail audit` relies on them being committed.

### 5. COMPLETE (LAST thing, after committing)
```bash
agentrail complete --summary "what you accomplished" \
  --reward 1 \
  --actions "tools and approach used"
```
- If the step failed: `--reward -1 --failure-mode "what went wrong"`
- If the saga is finished: add `--done`
- To define the next step in the same call: add
  `--next-slug <slug> --next-prompt "<prompt>"`

### 6. STOP (after complete, DO NOT continue working)
Do NOT make further code changes after running `agentrail complete`.
Any changes after complete are untracked and invisible to the next
session. Future work belongs in the NEXT step, not this one.

## Key Rules

- **Do NOT skip steps** -- the next session depends on accurate tracking
- **Do NOT ask for permission** -- the step prompt is the instruction
- **Do NOT continue working** after `agentrail complete`
- **Commit before complete** -- always commit first, then record completion
- **No step → no work** -- if `agentrail next` shows no step, stop and ask

## CRITICAL: `.agentrail/` directory rules

The `.agentrail/` directory is the durable record of saga and step
history. Treat it like source code. Past incidents in this repo lost
work because these rules were not followed.

- **`.agentrail/` MUST be tracked in git.** Never add it to
  `.gitignore`. If you find it ignored, that is a bug -- unignore it
  and commit the existing contents first.
- **NEVER edit or delete files under `.agentrail/` or
  `.agentrail-archive/` by hand.** No `rm`, `rm -rf`, `mv`, or
  `Write`/`Edit` tool calls on anything inside these directories.
  Always go through agentrail subcommands (`init`, `add`, `begin`,
  `complete`, `abort`, `archive`, `plan`).
- Direct deletion of untracked step files is **unrecoverable** --
  git reflog cannot restore blobs that were never staged. This has
  happened before and lost saga history.
- **Commit `.agentrail/` changes alongside the code changes that
  produced them**, not in a separate "tracking" commit later.
- Before any risky agent operation that touches `.agentrail/` (a
  big run, a rebase, cleaning untracked files), run
  `agentrail snapshot` to save a recoverable copy into the git
  object store.

## Useful Commands

```bash
agentrail status          # Current saga state (read-only)
agentrail history         # All completed steps (read-only)
agentrail plan            # View the plan
agentrail next            # Current step + context

# Maintenance / ad-hoc
agentrail add --slug <slug> --prompt "<prompt>"   # add a step without
                                                  # completing the current one
agentrail abort --reason "..."                    # mark current step blocked
agentrail archive --reason "..."                  # close out a saga
agentrail init <name>                             # start a new saga

# Diagnostics and recovery
agentrail audit                  # report saga-vs-git gaps (markdown)
agentrail audit --emit-commands  # shell script of suggested `add` lines
                                 # for orphan commits (review before run)
agentrail snapshot               # save .agentrail/ into git object store
agentrail snapshot --list        # list snapshot refs
```

If `agentrail audit` reports orphan commits or working-tree drift,
fix it before starting new work. Use `--emit-commands` to scaffold
retroactive `add` lines, then review and run.

- Before any risky agent operation that touches `.agentrail/` (a
  big run, a rebase, cleaning untracked files), run
  `agentrail snapshot` to save a recoverable copy into the git
  object store.

## Useful Commands

```bash
agentrail status          # Current saga state (read-only)
agentrail history         # All completed steps (read-only)
agentrail plan            # View the plan
agentrail next            # Current step + context

# Maintenance / ad-hoc
agentrail add --slug <slug> --prompt "<prompt>"   # add a step without
                                                  # completing the current one
agentrail abort --reason "..."                    # mark current step blocked
agentrail archive --reason "..."                  # close out a saga
agentrail init <name>                             # start a new saga

# Diagnostics and recovery
agentrail audit                  # report saga-vs-git gaps (markdown)
agentrail audit --emit-commands  # shell script of suggested `add` lines
                                 # for orphan commits (review before run)
agentrail snapshot               # save .agentrail/ into git object store
agentrail snapshot --list        # list snapshot refs
```

If `agentrail audit` reports orphan commits or working-tree drift,
fix it before starting new work. Use `--emit-commands` to scaffold
retroactive `add` lines, then review and run.

# sw-cor24-plsw -- Claude Instructions

## Project Overview

PL/SW (Programming Language for Software Wrighter) compiler for the
COR24 24-bit RISC ISA. A freestanding, PL/I-inspired systems language
with AST-aware compile-time macros, transpiling to COR24 assembler
source (.s). Written in C (via tc24r cross-compiler), targeting the
COR24 emulator.

See `docs/` for detailed documentation:
- `docs/prd.md` -- requirements
- `docs/architecture.md` -- system architecture
- `docs/design.md` -- design decisions
- `docs/plan.md` -- phased implementation plan
- `docs/usage.md` -- language reference and usage guide
- `docs/research.txt` -- PL/SW design research and rationale

## CRITICAL: AgentRail Session Protocol (MUST follow exactly)

### 1. START (do this FIRST, before anything else)
```bash
agentrail next
```
Read the output carefully. It contains your current step, prompt,
plan context, and any relevant skills/trajectories.

### 2. BEGIN (immediately after reading the next output)
```bash
agentrail begin
```

### 3. WORK (do what the step prompt says)
Do NOT ask "want me to proceed?". The step prompt IS your instruction.
Execute it directly.

### 4. COMMIT (after the work is done)
Commit your code changes with git. Use `/mw-cp` for the checkpoint
process (pre-commit checks, docs, detailed commit, push).

### 5. COMPLETE (LAST thing, after committing)
```bash
agentrail complete --summary "what you accomplished" \
  --reward 1 \
  --actions "tools and approach used"
```
- If the step failed: `--reward -1 --failure-mode "what went wrong"`
- If the saga is finished: add `--done`

### 6. STOP (after complete, DO NOT continue working)
Do NOT make further code changes after running `agentrail complete`.
Any changes after complete are untracked and invisible to the next
session. Future work belongs in the NEXT step, not this one.

## Key Rules

- **Do NOT skip steps** -- the next session depends on accurate tracking
- **Do NOT ask for permission** -- the step prompt is the instruction
- **Do NOT continue working** after `agentrail complete`
- **Commit before complete** -- always commit first, then record completion

## Useful Commands

```bash
agentrail status          # Current saga state
agentrail history         # All completed steps
agentrail plan            # View the plan
agentrail next            # Current step + context
```

## Build / Test

This project uses tc24r (C compiler) and cor24-run (emulator):

```bash
# Compile C to COR24 assembly
tc24r src/plsw.c -o build/plsw.s

# Assemble and run on emulator
cor24-run --run build/plsw.s
```

Stable toolchain references:
- `~/github/sw-vibe-coding/tc24r` -- C compiler source
- `~/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-emulator` -- emulator + assembler
- `~/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-tinyc` -- tc24r compiler (Rust)

Sibling language projects for reference:
- `~/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-apl` -- APL interpreter (C via tc24r)
- `~/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-pascal` -- Pascal compiler (C via tc24r)
- `~/github/sw-vibe-coding/tml24c` -- Macro Lisp compiler (C via tc24r)

## COR24 Quick Reference

- 24-bit RISC, 8 registers (r0-r2 GP, fp, sp, z, iv, ir)
- 1 MB SRAM + 8 KB EBR stack
- UART at 0xFF0100 (data), 0xFF0101 (status)
- LED at 0xFF0000
- No hardware divide -- use software division
- Variable-length instructions (1, 2, or 4 bytes)
- Calling convention: args on stack (R-to-L), return in r0, link via stack
- Prologue: push fp, push r2, push r1, mov fp,sp
- First arg at fp+9 (3 saved regs x 3 bytes)

## PL/SW Language Summary

- Source files: `.plsw`, macro files: `.msw`
- PL/I-like syntax: DCL, PROC, DO/END, IF/THEN/ELSE
- Integer-only types: BIT, BYTE, WORD, INT(n), CHAR, PTR
- Level-based structure declarations (1, 3, 5, ...)
- Macro invocation: `?MACRO(KEYWORD(value), ...)`
- Inline assembly: `ASM DO; "instr"; END;`
- No floats, no runtime, freestanding

+that `agentrail audit` cannot reconcile.
### 2. BEGIN (immediately after reading the next output)

### 3. WORK (do what the step prompt says)
Do NOT ask "want me to proceed?". The step prompt IS your instruction.

### 6. STOP (after complete, DO NOT continue working)

+## CRITICAL: `.agentrail/` directory rules
+The `.agentrail/` directory is the durable record of saga and step
+history. Treat it like source code. Past incidents in this repo lost
+work because these rules were not followed.
+- **`.agentrail/` MUST be tracked in git.** Never add it to
+  `.gitignore`. If you find it ignored, that is a bug -- unignore it
+  and commit the existing contents first.
+- **NEVER edit or delete files under `.agentrail/` or
+  `.agentrail-archive/` by hand.** No `rm`, `rm -rf`, `mv`, or
+  `Write`/`Edit` tool calls on anything inside these directories.
+  Always go through agentrail subcommands (`init`, `add`, `begin`,
+  `complete`, `abort`, `archive`, `plan`).
+- Direct deletion of untracked step files is **unrecoverable** --
+  git reflog cannot restore blobs that were never staged. This has
+  happened before and lost saga history.
+- **Commit `.agentrail/` changes alongside the code changes that

+  object store.
## Useful Commands

+agentrail history         # All completed steps (read-only)
agentrail plan            # View the plan
agentrail next            # Current step + context

+agentrail abort --reason "..."                    # mark current step blocked
+agentrail archive --reason "..."                  # close out a saga
+agentrail init <name>                             # start a new saga
+# Diagnostics and recovery
+agentrail audit                  # report saga-vs-git gaps (markdown)
+agentrail audit --emit-commands  # shell script of suggested `add` lines
+                                 # for orphan commits (review before run)
+agentrail snapshot               # save .agentrail/ into git object store
+agentrail snapshot --list        # list snapshot refs
+If `agentrail audit` reports orphan commits or working-tree drift,
+fix it before starting new work. Use `--emit-commands` to scaffold
+retroactive `add` lines, then review and run.

git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
chore(agentrail): commit plsw-modular saga + archive v1, expand CLAUDE.md

Two long-overdue housekeeping changes that an `agentrail audit` run
turned up:

1. Commit the v1->v2 saga transition that has been sitting in the
   working tree since 2026-04-07. The v1 plsw-compiler saga (67 steps,
   35 sessions) was archived to .agentrail-archive/plsw-compiler-
   20260407T095901/ when plsw-modular was created, but the cleanup of
   .agentrail/ was never committed. Also commit plsw-modular's own
   step directories (5 steps, completed) and its single session file.
   git correctly tracks the v1 files as renames into the archive dir,
   so this is a no-op for content -- it just brings git's view in sync
   with reality.

2. Expand CLAUDE.md's agentrail section with the rules that would have
   prevented the gaps `agentrail audit` is currently flagging:
   - Stop and ask the user if `agentrail next` reports no current step
     (do NOT invent ad-hoc work between sagas).
   - NEVER edit/delete files under .agentrail/ or .agentrail-archive/
     by hand. Always go through agentrail subcommands.
   - Commit .agentrail/ files in the same commit as the code changes
     that produced them.
   - Don't expand step scope.
   - Document the new diagnostic / recovery commands: `audit`,
     `audit --emit-commands`, `snapshot`, `snapshot --list`, `add`,
     `abort`, `archive`.

A safety snapshot of the pre-commit state lives at
refs/agentrail/snapshots/20260411T111735.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

input.content:
# PL/SW Maintenance Saga

Ongoing maintenance saga for the PL/SW compiler after the v1
(plsw-compiler, 67 steps) and v2 (plsw-modular, 5 steps) sagas
completed. This saga has two phases:

## Phase 1 -- Retroactive bookkeeping

Bind the post-modular orphan commits (Apr 7 - Apr 10 2026) to step
records so `agentrail audit` reports a clean repo. These were
ad-hoc commits made between sagas because the old CLAUDE.md did
not tell agents to stop when no current step exists. The CLAUDE.md
gap is now fixed.

Orphan commits to absorb:
- 72a7111 fix(plsw): raise %DEFINE table to 512 (Apr 7 09:57)
- 85a58f3 docs(readme): add Projects Using PL/SW section (Apr 7 17:25)
- e43f01a feat(runtime): add _UART_GETCHAR stub for UART RX (Apr 8 20:05)
- e7d4805 feat(select): add SELECT/WHEN/OTHERWISE control flow (Apr 10 18:29)
- b63ab15 fix(plsw): make OTHERWISE optional in SELECT (Apr 10 20:52)

## Phase 2 -- Ongoing maintenance

Track future bug fixes, small features, doc updates, and any other
work that does not warrant a dedicated saga. Each step is one
focused change. When a coherent multi-step initiative comes along
(e.g. another major feature like modular compilation was), archive
this saga and start a dedicated one.

Open work as of saga creation:
- GitHub issue #33: long IF/ELSE-IF chain limit -- documented as
  known limitation, no fix planned. Tracking ticket only.

## Rules

- Each step is one commit (or one cohesive set of commits when work
  needs to be staged).
- Use `agentrail add` for retroactive entries (Phase 1) since the
  commits already exist.
- Use the normal `next` / `begin` / `complete` flow for forward work.
- Stop and ask the user before doing any code work if `agentrail
  next` reports no current step.

agentrail add \
  --slug select-otherwise-optional \
  --prompt "Fix GitHub issue #34: SELECT/WHEN should compile when OTHERWISE is omitted. Root cause is that nd_alloc initializes nd_ival to 0, but NODE_NULL is -1, so cg_select's check for an absent OTHERWISE body always fails and runs cg_stmt(0) on whatever node 0 happens to be." \
  --commit b63ab151ecbc338d290448ff4ab3825d3b7f4b70 2>&1 \
&& agentrail begin 2>&1 \
&& agentrail complete --reward 1 \
   --summary "Fixed SELECT-without-OTHERWISE bug (issue #34). One-line fix in src/parser.h: explicitly set nd_ival[n] = NODE_NULL when allocating the NODE_SELECT, so cg_select correctly detects the absent OTHERWISE branch. Verified all three cases: matching WHEN, fall-through no-match, non-MAIN proc." \
   --actions "Read parser.h SELECT branch and codegen.h cg_select; trace nd_alloc initialization vs NODE_NULL value; add nd_ival[n] = NODE_NULL after nd_alloc in parse_stmt; verify with three synthetic test programs" 2>&1
